text.title: Scaling and Deployment for Monoliths
text.summary: How to scale a monolith horizontally, requirements for statelessness, and deployment strategies (blue-green, canary, feature flags) that reduce deployment risk.

rule.statement: A monolith can be scaled horizontally behind a load balancer only if it is stateless: no in-memory sessions, no local file system writes for shared data, no sticky connections for business logic. Externalize all shared state before adding instances.
rule.rationale: Sticky sessions appear to solve stateful monolith scaling, but they create uneven load distribution and become a single-point failure per user session. Stateless architecture distributes requests freely and handles instance loss without session loss.

list.item: Sessions: store in Redis or database, not in-process memory
Uploaded files: store in S3/GCS/object storage, not local filesystem
Background job queues: use external broker (Celery+Redis, Sidekiq+Redis), not in-process threads that die with the instance
WebSocket connections: use sticky sessions only for the socket layer; business logic remains stateless
Database connections: use a connection pool (PgBouncer) to prevent connection count scaling with instance count

rule.statement: Use blue-green deployments for the monolith to achieve zero-downtime deploys. Use feature flags for high-risk functionality changes so the code ships without activating the behavior. Use canary deployments to validate new versions on a percentage of traffic before full rollout.
rule.rationale: Monolithic deployments replace the entire application; a bug in any part affects all users. Deployment strategies decouple "code in production" from "feature active in production", enabling risk-staged rollouts and instant rollback without redeployment.

list.item: Blue-green: two identical environments; traffic shifts instantly; rollback = shift traffic back — requires 2x infrastructure during transition
Canary: new version receives 5%→25%→100% of traffic; monitor error rate at each step; automated rollback on threshold breach
Feature flags: code ships dark; flag enables feature for a user segment; rollback = disable flag — no redeployment needed
Rolling update: replace instances one at a time; requires backward-compatible database migrations (never break old code with a new migration)

antipattern.description: Deploying database migrations and application code in a single step
antipattern.reason: If the migration fails mid-deploy, the application is in an inconsistent state. Always deploy migrate-then-upgrade: migration first (backward-compatible), then new application version.
